cards.name: One-In Ladder Run
cards.cardSummary: One-In Ladder Run uses the ladder to build clean single-contact foot rhythm, posture, and sprint-like front-side mechanics. Best used as a crisp, low-volume skill or power exposure rather than a fatigue circuit.
cards.bestPlacement: Early in the session after Raise/Mobilize work; pair with sprint mechanics, COD teaching, hopping prep, or hand-support prep depending on the pattern.
cards.description: One-In Ladder Run is a ladder-focused exercise for clean single-contact foot rhythm, posture, and sprint-like front-side mechanics.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary phase: Movement Intelligence. Subrole: locomotion_sprint_mechanics. Treat the ladder as a constraint for precision; progress speed only after clean rhythm, posture, and spacing.
cards.athleteLanguage: Fast feet are only good if they are clean feet.
cards.whyItWorks: The ladder creates clear visual boxes that constrain stride length, contact location, and rhythm. This lets coaches shape clean single-contact foot rhythm, posture, and sprint-like front-side mechanics without heavy fatigue, while giving athletes immediate feedback when they miss a box or lose posture.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Movement Intelligence under locomotion_sprint_mechanics: it teaches rhythm and coordination while the nervous system is fresh, before fatigue turns learning into conditioning.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not turn this into a long conditioning circuit. If posture, rhythm, or foot accuracy breaks down, the drill has outlived its purpose for that set.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by pattern complexity, then by speed, then by impact or reactive demand. Never progress a ladder drill by simply making it messier or longer.

movementRequirements.balance_demand: dynamic balance over changing foot positions
movementRequirements.coordination_demand: rapid foot placement, rhythm, and spatial awareness

coachingExecution.movement_description: One-In Ladder Run uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train clean single-contact foot rhythm, posture, and sprint-like front-side mechanics. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Lay the agility ladder flat and remove tripping hazards.
Show one slow walk-through rep before speed is allowed.
Start tall with eyes forward, arms ready, and feet just outside the first box.
execution_steps: Step through the ladder using the assigned foot pattern.
Keep contacts light, posture tall, and arms coordinated with the feet.
Finish the ladder cleanly, decelerate under control, and walk back for the next rep.
athlete_cues: Slow down if your feet get messy.
breathing_cues: Breathe naturally; do not hold breath to move faster.
common_faults: Torso folding or twisting to chase speed
quality_gate: No missed boxes for one full pass
Posture stays tall
Speed increases only after the pattern is clean
stop_signs: Athlete cannot decelerate after the finish

scaling.youth_beginner: Walk the pattern first; shorten to 4-5 boxes and use light rhythm instead of speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the full ladder at controlled speed; speed up only after a clean pass.
scaling.teen: Use crisp passes with eyes up and arms coordinated; add sprint exit only when appropriate.
scaling.adult_beginner: Reduce speed and keep rest high enough for coordination quality.
scaling.adult_advanced: Increase cadence, add a clean exit, or pair with a sprint mechanic drill.
scaling.older_adult: Use step patterns, wider spacing, and no race pressure.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use low-impact walking versions; avoid breath-holding, rushing, or high-impact contacts.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based adjustment; scale by coordination, speed quality, pain history, joint tolerance, and attention demand.
doNotUseWhen: Athlete is too fatigued to keep clean contacts
Ladder becomes a trip hazard or slides on the surface
Pain appears with the required foot, hop, or hand-support pattern
mediaInternalNotes: Film one slow demonstration and one clean normal-speed rep from the front or diagonal angle.
Show the start position, full ladder pass, and deceleration/reset after the finish.
Keep YouTube links in mediaReferences, not internal notes, so Client view can embed them.

difficultyProfile.notes: Typical fit for ages 6+ when the athlete can follow the ladder pattern without tripping or rushing.

readiness_checks: Athlete can walk the pattern without tripping
Training surface is dry and ladder lies flat
No pain with the intended foot, hop, or hand-support pattern
contraindications: Acute ankle, knee, hip, back, wrist, or shoulder pain that worsens with the drill

coachingExecution.movement_description: Forward-Backward Quick Feet uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train forward and retreating rhythm with posture preserved during direction changes. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Lay the agility ladder flat and remove tripping hazards.
Show one slow walk-through rep before speed is allowed.
Start tall with eyes forward, arms ready, and feet just outside the first box.
execution_steps: Step through the ladder using the assigned foot pattern.
Keep contacts light, posture tall, and arms coordinated with the feet.
Finish the ladder cleanly, decelerate under control, and walk back for the next rep.
athlete_cues: Slow down if your feet get messy.
breathing_cues: Breathe naturally; do not hold breath to move faster.
common_faults: Torso folding or twisting to chase speed
quality_gate: No missed boxes for one full pass
Posture stays tall
Speed increases only after the pattern is clean
stop_signs: Athlete cannot decelerate after the finish

scaling.youth_beginner: Walk the pattern first; shorten to 4-5 boxes and use light rhythm instead of speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the full ladder at controlled speed; speed up only after a clean pass.
scaling.teen: Use crisp passes with eyes up and arms coordinated; add sprint exit only when appropriate.
scaling.adult_beginner: Reduce speed and keep rest high enough for coordination quality.
scaling.adult_advanced: Increase cadence, add a clean exit, or pair with a sprint mechanic drill.
scaling.older_adult: Use step patterns, wider spacing, and no race pressure.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use low-impact walking versions; avoid breath-holding, rushing, or high-impact contacts.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based adjustment; scale by coordination, speed quality, pain history, joint tolerance, and attention demand.
doNotUseWhen: Athlete is too fatigued to keep clean contacts
Ladder becomes a trip hazard or slides on the surface
Pain appears with the required foot, hop, or hand-support pattern
mediaInternalNotes: Film one slow demonstration and one clean normal-speed rep from the front or diagonal angle.
Show the start position, full ladder pass, and deceleration/reset after the finish.
Keep YouTube links in mediaReferences, not internal notes, so Client view can embed them.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Lateral Quick Step to Stick uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train quick lateral rhythm followed by a controlled stick and quiet landing. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Place the ladder flat on a non-slip surface.
Begin just before the first box with knees soft and eyes forward.
Choose two-foot or single-leg stance exactly as prescribed.
execution_steps: Hop through the boxes using the pattern while keeping contacts quiet.
Land with foot, knee, and hip stacked; avoid knees diving inward.
Reset after the ladder or after each stick before the next rep.
athlete_cues: Spring off the ground, do not stomp.
breathing_cues: Exhale naturally on effort; avoid bracing so hard that breath stops.
common_faults: Hopping too far beyond the box
quality_gate: Contacts stay quiet
Knee tracks well
Landing can be held for two seconds when required

scaling.youth_beginner: Reduce to line hops or step-in/step-out pattern; require quiet landings before adding speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use small hops through 4-6 boxes with a stick every third contact.
scaling.teen: Use prescribed contacts with full reset between passes; progress only if knee tracking stays clean.
scaling.adult_beginner: Keep amplitude low and use two-foot versions before single-leg versions.
scaling.adult_advanced: Increase speed or single-leg demand gradually; keep contacts capped and quality high.
scaling.older_adult: Use step patterns or very low hops only if joint tolerance and balance are strong.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Prefer low-impact step patterns; avoid repeated hopping unless specifically cleared and symptom-free.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Forward Icky to Sprint Exit uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train turn a clean ladder rhythm into a sharp first step and acceleration exit. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Lay the agility ladder flat and remove tripping hazards.
Show one slow walk-through rep before speed is allowed.
Start tall with eyes forward, arms ready, and feet just outside the first box.
execution_steps: Step through the ladder using the assigned foot pattern.
Keep contacts light, posture tall, and arms coordinated with the feet.
Finish the ladder cleanly, decelerate under control, and walk back for the next rep.
athlete_cues: Slow down if your feet get messy.
breathing_cues: Breathe naturally; do not hold breath to move faster.
common_faults: Torso folding or twisting to chase speed
quality_gate: No missed boxes for one full pass
Posture stays tall
Speed increases only after the pattern is clean
stop_signs: Athlete cannot decelerate after the finish

scaling.youth_beginner: Walk the pattern first; shorten to 4-5 boxes and use light rhythm instead of speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the full ladder at controlled speed; speed up only after a clean pass.
scaling.teen: Use crisp passes with eyes up and arms coordinated; add sprint exit only when appropriate.
scaling.adult_beginner: Reduce speed and keep rest high enough for coordination quality.
scaling.adult_advanced: Increase cadence, add a clean exit, or pair with a sprint mechanic drill.
scaling.older_adult: Use step patterns, wider spacing, and no race pressure.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use low-impact walking versions; avoid breath-holding, rushing, or high-impact contacts.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based adjustment; scale by coordination, speed quality, pain history, joint tolerance, and attention demand.
doNotUseWhen: Athlete is too fatigued to keep clean contacts

Pain appears with the required foot, hop, or hand-support pattern
mediaInternalNotes: Film one slow demonstration and one clean normal-speed rep from the front or diagonal angle.
Show the start position, full ladder pass, and deceleration/reset after the finish.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Ladder Cut-Out to 5-Yard Acceleration uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train finish technical footwork with one decisive cut and a short acceleration burst. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Lay the agility ladder flat and remove tripping hazards.
Show one slow walk-through rep before speed is allowed.
Start tall with eyes forward, arms ready, and feet just outside the first box.
execution_steps: Step through the ladder using the assigned foot pattern.
Keep contacts light, posture tall, and arms coordinated with the feet.
Finish the ladder cleanly, decelerate under control, and walk back for the next rep.
athlete_cues: Slow down if your feet get messy.
breathing_cues: Breathe naturally; do not hold breath to move faster.
common_faults: Torso folding or twisting to chase speed
quality_gate: No missed boxes for one full pass
Posture stays tall
Speed increases only after the pattern is clean
stop_signs: Athlete cannot decelerate after the finish

scaling.youth_beginner: Walk the pattern first; shorten to 4-5 boxes and use light rhythm instead of speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the full ladder at controlled speed; speed up only after a clean pass.
scaling.teen: Use crisp passes with eyes up and arms coordinated; add sprint exit only when appropriate.
scaling.adult_beginner: Reduce speed and keep rest high enough for coordination quality.
scaling.adult_advanced: Increase cadence, add a clean exit, or pair with a sprint mechanic drill.
scaling.older_adult: Use step patterns, wider spacing, and no race pressure.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use low-impact walking versions; avoid breath-holding, rushing, or high-impact contacts.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based adjustment; scale by coordination, speed quality, pain history, joint tolerance, and attention demand.
doNotUseWhen: Athlete is too fatigued to keep clean contacts

coachingExecution.movement_description: Two-Foot Hop Each Box uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train basic elastic contacts and quiet two-foot landings through each box. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Place the ladder flat on a non-slip surface.
Begin just before the first box with knees soft and eyes forward.
Choose two-foot or single-leg stance exactly as prescribed.
execution_steps: Hop through the boxes using the pattern while keeping contacts quiet.
Land with foot, knee, and hip stacked; avoid knees diving inward.
Reset after the ladder or after each stick before the next rep.
athlete_cues: Spring off the ground, do not stomp.
breathing_cues: Exhale naturally on effort; avoid bracing so hard that breath stops.
common_faults: Hopping too far beyond the box
quality_gate: Contacts stay quiet
Knee tracks well
Landing can be held for two seconds when required

scaling.youth_beginner: Reduce to line hops or step-in/step-out pattern; require quiet landings before adding speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use small hops through 4-6 boxes with a stick every third contact.
scaling.teen: Use prescribed contacts with full reset between passes; progress only if knee tracking stays clean.
scaling.adult_beginner: Keep amplitude low and use two-foot versions before single-leg versions.
scaling.adult_advanced: Increase speed or single-leg demand gradually; keep contacts capped and quality high.
scaling.older_adult: Use step patterns or very low hops only if joint tolerance and balance are strong.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Prefer low-impact step patterns; avoid repeated hopping unless specifically cleared and symptom-free.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based adjustment; scale by coordination, speed quality, pain history, joint tolerance, and attention demand.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Coach Call-Out Ladder Pattern uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train listen, choose the correct pattern, and move crisply without guessing. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Lay the agility ladder flat and remove tripping hazards.
Show one slow walk-through rep before speed is allowed.
Start tall with eyes forward, arms ready, and feet just outside the first box.
execution_steps: Step through the ladder using the assigned foot pattern.
Keep contacts light, posture tall, and arms coordinated with the feet.
Finish the ladder cleanly, decelerate under control, and walk back for the next rep.
athlete_cues: Slow down if your feet get messy.
breathing_cues: Breathe naturally; do not hold breath to move faster.
common_faults: Torso folding or twisting to chase speed
quality_gate: No missed boxes for one full pass
Posture stays tall
Speed increases only after the pattern is clean
stop_signs: Athlete cannot decelerate after the finish

scaling.youth_beginner: Use one call option only; walk the pattern before adding reaction speed.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use two call options and cap reps before focus drops.
scaling.teen: Use 2-3 call options and demand clean deceleration after each rep.
scaling.adult_beginner: Reduce cognitive load to one cue and one movement pattern.
scaling.adult_advanced: Add sport-specific cue, ball task, or exit direction while keeping reps fresh.
scaling.older_adult: Use predictable call timing and step patterns; avoid chase or collision tasks.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use low-impact step patterns and avoid competitive contact/chase formats.

cards.name: Bear Crawl Ladder
cards.cardSummary: Bear Crawl Ladder uses the ladder to build contralateral crawling rhythm, wrist/shoulder resilience, and trunk control. Best used as a crisp, low-volume skill or power exposure rather than a fatigue circuit.
cards.bestPlacement: Early in the session after Raise/Mobilize work; pair with sprint mechanics, COD teaching, hopping prep, or hand-support prep depending on the pattern.
cards.description: Bear Crawl Ladder is a ladder-focused exercise for contralateral crawling rhythm, wrist/shoulder resilience, and trunk control.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary phase: Resilience. Subrole: scapular_wrist_hand_support_resilience. Treat the ladder as a constraint for precision; progress speed only after clean rhythm, posture, and spacing.
cards.athleteLanguage: Fast feet are only good if they are clean feet.
cards.whyItWorks: The ladder creates clear visual boxes that constrain stride length, contact location, and rhythm. This lets coaches shape contralateral crawling rhythm, wrist/shoulder resilience, and trunk control without heavy fatigue, while giving athletes immediate feedback when they miss a box or lose posture.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Resilience under scapular_wrist_hand_support_resilience: it builds control, braking quality, and tissue tolerance rather than max speed.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not race the drill. The target is control under the ladder constraint; speed is only added after clean positions are repeatable.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by pattern complexity, then by speed, then by impact or reactive demand. Never progress a ladder drill by simply making it messier or longer.

movementRequirements.breathing_demand: controlled nasal breathing under trunk tension
movementRequirements.balance_demand: quadruped or plank base with hand-foot coordination
movementRequirements.coordination_demand: contralateral sequencing and trunk stiffness

coachingExecution.movement_description: Bear Crawl Ladder uses the agility ladder as a visual target to train contralateral crawling rhythm, wrist/shoulder resilience, and trunk control. The athlete moves through the boxes with clean contacts, controlled posture, and enough rest to keep every rep crisp.
setup: Place the agility ladder flat and clear space around it.
Start in a strong bear crawl or plank position with hands near the first box.
Brace the trunk before moving; spread fingers and press the floor away.
execution_steps: Move hands, or hands and feet, through each ladder box using the assigned pattern.
Keep hips quiet and shoulders stacked over strong hands.
Finish the ladder, reset posture, and walk back before the next rep.
coach_cues: Move like a table, not a snake.
breathing_cues: Breathe behind the brace; do not hold breath through the whole ladder.
common_faults: Hands landing outside the target boxes
quality_gate: No low-back sag
Hands hit boxes cleanly
Athlete can stop instantly without collapsing
stop_signs: Loss of plank/bear position for more than one step

scaling.youth_beginner: Shorten the ladder to 3-4 boxes; allow knees down if plank strength is not ready.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use full ladder with slow tempo and a two-second posture freeze at the end.
scaling.teen: Use full ladder, controlled tempo, and add a pause if hips sway.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use bear crawl instead of full plank if shoulders or trunk fatigue early.
scaling.adult_advanced: Add a slower tempo, longer ladder, or clean transition into a push-up only if posture stays perfect.
scaling.older_adult: Use elevated hands on a bench with taped ladder marks; avoid floor if wrist or shoulder tolerance is limited.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Avoid prone bracing if uncomfortable; use elevated quadruped hand taps or standing ladder feet instead.
